body: SCREENING FOR REFERRAL TO E&T The State agency must screen each work registrant to determine if it is appropriate, based on State-specific criteria, to refer them to the E&T program per 7 CFR 273.7 (c)(2). State agencies may operate program components in which individuals elect to participate, per 7 CFR 273.7(e)(4). List the State-specific criteria eligibility workers use to screen individuals to determine if it is appropriate to refer them to the State's SNAP E&T program. The specific state criteria for referral to E&T in Colorado are: Is the individual within the appropriate age range? 16 or 17 years and the head of household, or 18-64 years old? Is there an available E&T program operating in the county of residence? Is the individual work-ready and willing to participate in an E&T program? All SNAP participants who meet the age requirements and reside in a county where E&T programs are in operation are eligible to participate in the Colorado E&T program. What information does the State provide to a SNAP recipient to explain SNAP E&T participation criteria? During the SNAP application and recertification process, eligibility workers explain the EF program, including who qualifies to participate based on age, county of residence, and readiness to engage in employment or training. Clients are informed that the program is voluntary and are given details on the services available, such as case management, job search support, and participant reimbursements. If referred, clients receive the name and contact information of the EF provider and may also be given written materials or flyers with additional program details. How does the State document that the information has been provided? Eligibility workers document these conversations and outcomes in CBMS, including on the Work Requirements screen and in case comments. What is the State's model for screening and referral to SNAP E&T? Select all that apply. Reverse Referral Direct Referral When does screening for referral to E&T occur? Select all that apply. Initial Certification Recertification Reported change in the work registrant status of households Other 22

body: Describe the process for screening for direct referral to E&T, including the staff involved. During the application and recertification interview, eligibility workers engage in a comprehensive conversation with clients about SNAP work requirements and the SNAP E&T programs available in the county of residence. Eligibility workers explain the SNAP work requirements and the impacts to eligibility, how the E&T program can help individuals maintain eligibility for SNAP, as well as provide resources to aid in securing and maintaining employment as well and career development. Eligibility staff will then assess the client’s ability and interest in participating. If the client meets the conditions of the state criteria for screening, eligibility workers will then send the referral to the most appropriate E&T provider in the county. The screening and referral process is led entirely by county eligibility workers in collaboration with EF POs and supported by state-provided tools and guidance. When does the screening for a reverse referral request occur? The screening for a reverse referral request occurs after an EF PO identifies a SNAP client who was not originally referred through the eligibility process but may benefit from or is interested in E&T services. Describe the process for screening during the reverse referral request process, including the staff involved. EF POs notify the client over the phone, in person, or in writing that a screening needs to be conducted by an eligibility worker. The eligibility worker will then follow the screening process and refer to the State-specific criteria. Are participants informed about participant reimbursements before the individual is referred to E&T by eligibility staff? Yes No How are participants informed about participant reimbursements? During the screening and referral process, eligibility workers inform clients of participant reimbursements (also known as supportive payments in Colorado) as part of their explanation of the EF program. Once referred, clients receive additional information and are offered participant reimbursements during their initial meeting and ongoing case management appointments with their EF PO worker. Clients can receive transportation assistance and other supportive payments to attach to the program before their first appointment with EF if this need is expressed during the eligibility screening during the initial application, recertification, status changes, or upon request. State E&T staff and county EF POs provide eligibility workers with a list of E&T supportive payments available in their county. This allows eligibility staff to provide information to clients on what supportive payments are available. 23

body: REFERRAL TO E&T In accordance with 7 CFR 273.7(c)(2), in order to participate in SNAP E&T, the State agency must make the referral. The referral method may vary from participant to participant. Orally Electronic Forms Physical Forms Emails Text Messages Other If the State receives a reverse referral request from an E&T provider, what steps does the State take? EF POs follow internal business processes to notify the SNAP client over the phone, in person, or in writing that a screening needs to be conducted by a county SNAP eligibility worker. Per State policy, the EF PO has 10 business days to refer the SNAP client to a county DHS eligibility team to be screened. The SNAP eligibility worker will then follow the screening and referral process outlined in this State plan. How does the State communicate to the SNAP participant that they are in SNAP E&T? Orally Electronic Forms Physical Forms Emails Text Messages Other How is information about the referral communicated to E&T providers, as applicable? SNAP eligibility workers communicate with EF POs when referrals are made through email, phone calls, or in-person meetings. And EF POs also communicate with SNAP eligibility workers when referrals result in successful program enrollment through email, phone calls, or in-person meetings. This communication method depends on the county DHS’s and EF PO’s business processes. The eligibility worker must make referrals to an EF program operator within 10 business days, and EF POs must take action on the referral within five business days (taking action includes contacting the SNAP recipient to schedule a one-on-one appointment or orientation). How is information about the referral communicated within the State agency? A client's referral status is updated in CBMS in real-time by the EF PO. When an eligibility worker refers a client to EF, the EF PO updates the client’s EF plan status to “EF referred”. When an EF PO accepts the referral and the client successfully enrolls in EF, the EF PO updates the client’s plan in CBMS to “EF Enrolled.” Clients who do not engage in EF or who decline program services are also documented in CBMS with the “ EF declined program services” status. In Person Virtually Online Self-Paced Other What happens during the orientation? Based on the EF PO's business model, orientation is held in a group setting or 1:1 with the client at an initial appointment. SNAP clients are provided with more specific information regarding what supportive services, employment, and training services are available to them. Clients are also informed of program expectations (monthly check-ins, how to engage and complete program services, etc.). Currently, orientations and appointments are held in person, over the phone, and/or virtually, depending upon the EF PO’s business model. 26

body: ASSESSMENT Does the State require or provide an assessment? Yes No Who conducts the assessment? Select all that apply. State Agency E&T Provider Self-Assessment Intermediary Local Office Other When are participants assessed? The comprehensive employability assessment is a critical step in the EF case management process and is completed by the EF PO during orientation or the initial appointment. Assessments are completed in person and/or virtually between the client and the EF PO by completing either the State's EF Comprehensive Employability Assessment and the Transferable Skills Checklist or an EF PO’s assessment tool that is approved by the State EF program team. Assessments help determine the appropriate E&T component (commonly referred to as a program service) the client can engage in, as well as assess for barriers to employment. Areas that are assessed include employment history, education, and the client’s short- and long-term goals. Assessments must be conducted at least annually; however, EF POs assess clients at least monthly for barriers to employment and the need for supportive payments or updates to the client's individual employment plan. Describe the assessment. List the tools used in the assessment. Both the comprehensive assessment and transferable skills checklist are used to assess different areas such as communication skills, planning skills, interpersonal skills, critical thinking skills, academic skills, and self-management skills. Results of an assessment and the supportive payments that are provided (from the EF program or a partner employment and training program) to meet a client’s needs are documented in CBMS. The client is provided with the results of their assessment as well. Does the assessment result in the completion of an individual employment plan? Yes No 27

body: How are assessment results shared with State agency staff? Select all that apply. Orally Electronic Forms Physical Forms MIS System Email Other Assessment is not shared with State agency staff Explain why assessment results are not shared with State agency staff. In a state-supervised, county-administered state, state agency staff do not provide direct case management services to clients. Because of this structural distinction, assessment results are not shared with state staff. Instead, assessment results are shared with EF PO case managers who are directly responsible for case management and service delivery. However, assessment results are recorded in CBMS case comments, and State agency staff have access to the assessment results at any time and review the assessment results during the state oversight process. The conciliation process is not a substitute for the determination of good cause when a client fails to comply. Does the State agency offer a conciliation process? Yes No 30

body: QUESTION RESPONSE FIELD How do E&T case managers coordinate with: SNAP eligibility staff EF POs share any information regarding potential work exemptions, good cause, and other case updates with county SNAP eligibility through the business process established by the county. Email correspondence and virtual/in-person meetings are the most common forms of communication/coordination. How do E&T case managers coordinate with: State E&T staff EF POs request technical assistance, staff cases, and submit reports and outcomes to State E&T staff. Email correspondence and virtual/in-person meetings are the most common forms of communication/coordination. How do E&T case managers coordinate with: Other E&T providers EF POs develop local-level collaborative meeting schedules, referral processes, provider determination communications, and co-enrollment strategies with other E&T providers. Email correspondence and virtual/in-person meetings are the most common forms of communication/coordination. How do E&T case managers coordinate with: Community resources EF POs develop local-level collaborative meeting schedules, referral processes, and co-enrollment strategies with community resources. Email correspondence and virtual/in-person meetings are the most common forms of communication/coordination. How does the State agency ensure E&T participants receive targeted case management services through an efficient administrative process, per 7 CFR 273.7(c)(6)(ii)? The State ensures all E&T participants receive targeted case management services through a combination of policy requirements, system tracking, and oversight. Each EF PO is required to describe their local case management approach in their annual Program Operator Plan, including how they provide individualized, client-centered case management and service delivery. All EF POs use the comprehensive employability assessment and an IEP to determine the services each participant needs to successfully engage in E&T components. These tools guide decisions about appropriate referrals, training, supportive services, and employment goals. The IEP must be updated at least every 120 days to ensure services remain relevant. At the administrative level, the State E&T team monitors compliance through data reports that track IEP engagement, component enrollment, and the issuance of supportive service payments. These reports allow the State to identify whether participants are receiving case management services at the required frequency and whether services are aligned with their assigned components. This process ensures targeted case management is consistently delivered across the state. 32
citation_label: Colorado SNAP E&T State Plan FFY 2026 32
heading: Page 32

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP E&T State Plan FFY 2026

body: How do your offered case management services support the participant in the E&T program and provide activities and resources that help the participant achieve program goals? Case management services are designed to be individualized and responsive to each client’s goals and barriers. EF POs conduct a comprehensive employability assessment and use that information to guide the development of an IEP, which captures the participant’s background, short- and long-term employment goals, responsibilities, referrals to resources, and action steps needed to reach their goals. The IEP and assessment together help determine the most appropriate services for each participant. For example, participants struggling to find employment during a supervised job search may be assessed for training opportunities. Individuals with justice involvement may receive support in obtaining background checks or referrals to reentry services. Participants enrolled in vocational training may only need monthly email check-ins, while others may require more frequent, face-to-face interaction. Supportive services, including participant reimbursements, are regularly discussed and offered during case management appointments to remove participation barriers. These tailored supports help ensure participants remain engaged and on track to meet their employment and training goals. How does the SNAP State agency ensure the case management services offered do not act as an impediment to successful participation in E&T? During the oversight process, technical assistance meetings, and training throughout the year, the State ensures case management is flexible, client-centered, and not overly burdensome. EF POs are encouraged to adjust the frequency and method of case management based on client needs and the component in which they are enrolled. For example, clients who are actively engaged in vocational training and making consistent progress may only need to check in monthly via email, rather than attending frequent in-person meetings. The use of monthly progress monitoring check-ins, the IEP, and the assessment allows case managers to tailor services without creating unnecessary administrative hurdles. Supportive services are proactively offered during each check-in to help remove barriers that could prevent successful participation. Furthermore, the State monitors case management frequency and delivery methods through administrative reports to identify and correct any practices that may inadvertently impede participation. 33
citation_label: Colorado SNAP E&T State Plan FFY 2026 33

body: GOOD CAUSE In accordance with 7 CFR 273.7(i), the State agency is responsible for determining good cause when a SNAP recipient fails or refuses to comply with SNAP work requirements. Since it is not possible for FNS to enumerate each individual situation that should or should not be considered good cause, the State agency must take into account the facts and circumstances, including information submitted by the employer and by the household member involved, in determining whether or not good cause exists. How does the State agency reach out to the SNAP participant to determine good cause? Select all that apply. Phone Call Email Text Message Physical Form MIS System How many attempts are made to reach out to the SNAP participant for additional information? One Two Three More than three 34

body: What is the State agency's criteria for good cause? Colorado SNAP regulations 4.308.1 outline the same good cause reasons featured in 7 CFR 273.7(i). 4.308.1 states: Upon a determination that the individual voluntarily quit employment, the local office shall determine if there was good cause, which shall include, but not be limited to, any of the following: A. Discrimination by an employer based on age, race, sex, color, handicap, religious beliefs, national origin, or political beliefs; B. Work demands or conditions that render continued employment unreasonable, such as working without being paid on schedule; C. Acceptance by the individual of employment, or enrollment of at least half-time in any recognized school, training program, or institution of higher education, that requires the individual to leave employment; D. Acceptance by any other household member of employment or enrollment at least half-time in any recognized school, training program, or institution of higher education in another county or political subdivision which requires the household to move and thereby requires the individual to leave employment; E. Resignations by persons under the age of sixty (60) that are recognized by the employer as retirement; F. Resignation from employment that does not meet the suitable criteria specified in Section 4.310.51; G. Because of circumstances beyond the control of the individual, accepted full-time employment subsequently either does not materialize or results in employment of less than thirty (30) hours a week or weekly earnings of less than the federal minimum wage multiplied by thirty (30) hours; H. Leaving a job in connection with patterns of employment in which workers frequently move from one employer to another, such as migrant farm labor or construction work, even though employment at the new site has not actually begun; I. Illness of the head of household; J. Illness of another household member requiring the presence of the head of household; K. A household emergency; L. The unavailability of transportation; or, M. Employer demands a reduction in the participant's work effort or salary due to no fault of the employee. N. Lack of adequate child care for children who have reached age 6 but are under age 12 Describe the State agency's process to determine good cause if there is not an appropriate and available opening for an E&T participant. Since Colorado is operating in a voluntary, non-ABAWD pledge SNAP E&T model, there are numerous openings for referrals from county SNAP eligibility workers. EF POs collaborate and develop shared business processes to communicate when an EF program is at capacity. For instance, if an external EF PO can only serve 50 clients for the year and they only have five available openings, they will communicate the number of openings over the phone, in writing, or in person to the appropriate county SNAP eligibility workers. As a result, the network is designed to ensure spots are available to all SNAP clients. The State E&T team convenes EF POs monthly to communicate program opportunities, program design, and appropriate and available openings. If an EF PO is close to or at capacity, another EF PO who provides similar services in the area could receive the referrals. Lastly, clients are also referred to local workforce centers and other E&T providers in the area. If an appropriate and available opening is not available, the client would be granted good cause. 35

body: PROVIDER DETERMINATIONS In accordance with 7 CFR 273.7(c)(18) a State agency must ensure that E&T providers are informed of their authority and responsibility to determine if an individual is ill-suited for a particular E&T component. Describe the process used by E&T providers to communicate provider determinations to the State agency. When an EF PO determines that a client is unable to participate in EF, the EF PO is required to notify the county SNAP eligibility team through email, phone, or an in-person meeting about a provider determination (PD) within ten days of the date the determination is made. The EF PO is required to document the PD in CBMS, including updating the client’s plan status to “EF Provider Determination” and detailed case comments. The EF PO may provide input on the most appropriate next steps for the client with a PD. Describe how the State agency notifies clients of a provider determination. As a state-supervised, county-administered state, the State agency is not responsible for notifying clients of a PD. This is the responsibility of the county's SNAP eligibility team. SNAP eligibility teams must notify the client of the PD within ten calendar days of receiving notification from the EF PO. This can be completed through a phone call attempt or a written notification that includes contact information for the client to call the county for further information. What is the timeframe for contacting clients after receiving a provider determination? 1-3 Days 4-7 Days 8-10 Days 36

body: DISQUALIFICATION POLICY FOR GENERAL WORK REQUIREMENTS This section applies to the General Work Requirements, not just to E&T, and should be completed by all States, regardless of whether they operate a mandatory or voluntary E&T program. All work registrants are subject to SNAP work requirements at 7 CFR 273.7(a). A nonexempt individual who refuses or fails to comply without good cause, as defined at 7 CFR 273.7(2), (3), and (4), with SNAP work requirements will be disqualified and subject to State disqualification periods. Noncompliance with SNAP work requirements includes voluntarily quitting a job or reducing work hours below 30 hours a month, and failing to comply with SNAP E&T (if assigned by the State agency). What period before application does the State agency use to determine voluntary quit and/or reduction in work effort without good cause per 7 CFR 273.7(j)(1)? 30 Days 60 Days For all occurrences of non-compliance discussed below, must the individual also comply to receive benefits again? Yes No For the first occurrence of non-compliance per 7 CFR 273.7(f)(2), the individual will be disqualified until the later of: One month or until the individual complies, as determined by the State agency Up to 3 months For the second occurrence of non-compliance per 7 CFR 273.7(f)(2)(ii), the individual will be disqualified until the later of: Three months or until the individual complies, as determined by the State agency Up to 6 months For the third or subsequent occurrence per 7 CFR 273.7(f)(2)(iii), the individual will be disqualified until the later of: 6 months or until the individual complies, as determined by the State agency A date determined by the State agency Permanently The State agency will disqualify the: Individual The entire household if the head of household is an ineligible individual 37

body: PARTICIPANT REIMBURSEMENTS In accordance with 7 CFR 273.7(d)(4), State agencies are required to pay for or reimburse participants for expenses that are reasonable, necessary, and directly related to participation in E&T. State agencies may impose a maximum limit for reimbursement payments. If a State agency serves mandatory E&T participants, it must meet all costs associated with mandatory participation. If an individual's expenses exceed those reimbursements available by the State agency, the individual must be placed into a suitable component or must be exempted from mandatory E&T. body: Explain how else childcare is paid for. Childcare payments vary by EF PO. All payment methods are offered, with the exception of a contract for dependent care. If dependent care agencies have a waiting list or otherwise cap the number of enrolled dependents, how will the State agency ensure E&T participants with dependent care needs receive dependent care? In cases where dependent care isn’t available, EF POs will inform clients to use a friend or family member to provide childcare. Friends and/or family members who are not a part of the client’s SNAP household are eligible to receive participant reimbursement payments for providing dependent care. How does the State agency ensure that the participant has the necessary participant reimbursements to begin participation in the E&T program? Through a combination of policy guidance, communication protocols, and participant-centered service delivery, the State ensures that all participants are equipped with the resources necessary to begin and sustain their participation in E&T. Eligibility workers and EF Program Operators (EF POs) are directed to inform clients of available participant reimbursements during the initial SNAP eligibility interview and again during the first EF appointment. This dual-point communication ensures that clients are aware of support services before beginning participation in E&T. To support immediate access to E&T services, EF POs use various methods to provide participant reimbursements in a timely and accessible manner. For example, many EF POs have bus passes or gas vouchers available on-site, or utilize EBT card loading to support transportation needs for clients attending their first EF appointment. Comprehensive employability assessments, conducted at the first appointment and throughout participation, identify and address logistical or financial barriers, including transportation, clothing, tools, or fees required to participate in components. This ensures that participant reimbursements are not only available at entry but are continually aligned with the client’s evolving needs. Additionally, EF POs use program marketing materials, such as flyers and intake forms, to communicate what reimbursements are available and how to request them. This helps normalize the availability of support services and empowers clients to access what they need to engage in the program from the outset. 50

body: WORK REGISTRANT DATA The SNAP general work requirements are described at 7 CFR 273.7(a). Individuals who do not meet a federal exemption from the general work requirements, as listed in 7 CFR 273.7(b)(1), are subject to the general work requirement and must register for work. In accordance with 7 CFR 273.7(c)(10), the State agency must submit to FNS the number of work registrants in the State as of October 1st. This information is submitted on the first quarter E&T Program Activity Report (FNS-583). Describe the process the State agency uses to count all work registrants in the State as of the first day of the new fiscal year (October 1). Colorado uses the CBMS to count all work registrants as of October 1, the first day of the new fiscal year. Work registrant status is determined by eligibility workers who assess each SNAP applicant’s or recipient’s work registration status during the eligibility determination process. CBMS captures this data and records the count of all work registrants in the system on October 1. This count serves as the baseline for the unduplicated annual count of work registrants. Provide information about how work registrant data is pulled from the eligibility system, including the date the data is pulled. Work registrant data is pulled directly from CBMS, the State’s eligibility system, on October 1st and at least once a quarter. The system records the number of work registrants on October 1 and continues to record unduplicated counts on a monthly basis, including again on October 1 each year. The State EF program team then compiles and reports these figures quarterly and annually on the FNS-583 report. How are work registrants identified in the eligibility system? Work registrants are identified in CBMS by eligibility workers during the SNAP eligibility determination process. The eligibility worker evaluates each applicant’s or recipient’s status and assigns work registration status accordingly. This status is then recorded in CBMS, allowing the system to track individuals required to register for work under SNAP guidelines. Describe measures taken to prevent duplicate counting of work registrants within the federal fiscal year. The initial count of work registrants is provided from CBMS through a work registration status assigned by decision tables for each SNAP applicant and recipient. CBMS takes an annual snapshot of names and social security numbers of all work registrants in the system on October 1st, which is reported to FNS. Every subsequent month, CBMS takes a snapshot of work registration codes, social security numbers, and names on the last day of the month. All new work registrants are tallied throughout the year; duplicated names or social security numbers will not be counted again. 51

body: NON-EDUCATION, NON-WORK COMPONENT: SUPERVISED JOB SEARCH Provide a summary of the State guidelines implementing supervised job search. EF Clients enrolled in SJS receive job search guidance and case management from trained EF PO program workers. State-approved locations in Colorado are: workforce centers, county offices, public libraries, county human services departments, TPP offices, and client residences (when equipped with the technology necessary to participate in the component). All State-approved locations must have computers, internet, or Wi-fi available to the client. An on-site phone and printer are preferred, but not required, to be at a State-approved location. Clients are allowed to participate in SJS at state-approved locations. They are also required to meet with an EF PO once a month, with a level of effort of at least 12 hours a month. Monthly meetings can be conducted virtually or in person. The goal of a monthly meeting is for the EF PO worker to provide feedback to the client on job search efforts and offer supportive payments that aid the client in obtaining sustainable employment. EF PO workers will ensure that SJS activities directly increase clients' employment opportunities by maintaining a working knowledge of the local labor market and understanding which job search tools are required for certain occupations (resume, application, cover letter, etc.). Trained EF PO workers will directly supervise SJS through: Video conferencing, over-the-phone meetings, regular (at least monthly) check-ins through email, text messages, and other forms of technology that are easily accessible to a client. In-person meetings at a State-approved location and messaging apps where trained EF PO workers can provide real-time feedback and assistance to clients may also be utilized. Monthly SJS activities and meetings will be documented in CBMS for each client, including the amount of time the client spent in the activity or meeting. Describe the direct path to employment. EF POs ensure SJS leads directly to employment by enrolling only job-ready clients who have been assessed during EF orientation. These clients have the basic skills, availability, and stability needed to actively seek and secure work. Once enrolled, clients receive job search guidance and case management from trained EF PO team members, who have current knowledge of the local labor market. EF POs help clients target real job opportunities by supporting tailored applications, resumes, and job search strategies specific to in-demand occupations in the community. Clients complete at least 12 hours of job search per month and meet monthly with their EF PO for feedback and support, including help with employment barriers. Meetings can be virtual or in person and are used to keep job search efforts focused and effective. SJS is supervised through regular check-ins, virtual communication, and meetings at State-approved locations with internet and computer access. All activities are tracked in CBMS. This structure ensures a clear, direct path from job search to employment. 58

body: NON-EDUCATION, NON-WORK COMPONENT: WORKFARE Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. Clients are required to work up to the number of hours equal to their SNAP allotment divided by the State minimum wage at a private, non-profit, or public organization. Clients are not required to work more hours monthly than the total number of hours obtained by dividing the household’s monthly food stamp allotment by the higher of the applicable federal or state minimum wage, up to 30 hours weekly, and will not exceed 120 hours per month. Job search as part of the workfare component can only be up to 30 days following certification of SNAP benefits. Workfare opportunities must be within an in-demand career within the local labor market. All workfare placements are subject to the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) and minimum wage laws apply. Indicate the Target Population this component will serve. Select all that apply. ABAWDs English Language Learners Homeless Individuals who need a HSD or GED Individuals who need a credential Individuals who need a new skill Individuals with disabilities Returning citizens (aka: ex-offenders) Single parents Students Those that reside in rural areas Underemployed Veterans Describe the criteria for participation. Include the skills, knowledge, or experience necessary for participation in the component. For example, literacy or numeracy levels, recent labor market attachment, computer literacy, etc. Individuals who exhibit work readiness and are interested in volunteering and/or gaining new skills while working with local non-profit organizations. It is preferred that individuals are interested in workfare opportunities that are in an in-demand career within the local area and can be demonstrated through: LMI Gateway analytics print-out O*NET Online employment outlook print-out Local job boards and/or Connecting Colorado Pre-employment offer letter Other documentation that supports the employment opportunity is in-demand or in a growing industry in the local labor market 61

body: EDUCATIONAL COMPONENT: BASIC / FOUNDATIONAL SKILLS INSTRUCTION Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. Clients can receive preparation and/or testing for the General Equivalency Diploma (GED) or equivalent. They can also receive pre-GED and basic skills education. EPB is also for clients who test between a 5.5 and 8.5 grade level with the goal of increasing their skill level and attaining a GED. Homework, classwork, and participation in the learning environment may also be assigned and counted as participation hours. The minimum level of effort is 12 hours a month in this component. Indicate the Target Population this component will serve. Select all that apply. ABAWDs English Language Learners Homeless Individuals who need a HSD or GED Individuals who need a credential Individuals who need a new skill Individuals with disabilities Returning citizens (aka: ex-offenders) Single parents Students Those that reside in rural areas Underemployed Veterans Describe the criteria for participation. Include the skills, knowledge, or experience necessary for participation in the component. For example, literacy or numeracy levels, recent labor market attachment, computer literacy, etc. Individuals with a TABE score ranging from a 5.5. to 8.5 grade level, or individuals that express a desire to obtain a GED. An individual within one of Colorado's eight targeted populations (in section VIII(c)) may benefit from this component if they do not have a High School Diploma or GED. Each EF PO will target the populations identified within their EF PO plans and place an individual in this component based on the results of the comprehensive employability assessment, and the client’s goals. Criteria: Can commit to the level of effort (at least 12 hours a month) Can communicate and produce progress updates for their EF PO upon request Can pursue and obtain employment or enter into another activity or into a WBL or WBL-SUB following completion of the EPB 63

Describe how the State agency ensures that costs attributed to the E&T program are not supplanting funds used for other existing educational programs. All costs are vetted using the "supplement not supplant" model. Meaning that federal funds cannot be used to perform a service that would normally be paid for with State or local funds. Clients are allowed to receive assistance with transportation, purchasing books, and other supportive payments based upon assessed need while attached to this component. When conducting a comprehensive employability assessment with a client, EF PO workers determine if other funding options can be used for the client’s education costs before E&T funds are used. EF PO workers are required to document this determination through a CBMS case comment, and provide appropriate documentation to further demonstrate that E&T funds are not supplanting non-federal funds when appropriate (such as an invoice or document that shows a student’s education is being paid by another funding source) and the State EF team will monitor and review for this evidence during the State’s ongoing oversight process. 64

body: Cost parity. Describe how the State agency ensures that costs charged to E&T do not exceed the costs charged for non-E&T participants. EF POs ensure that E&T participants and non-E&T participants are charged the same for services provided by vendors. Costs are prorated by program; EF is only invoiced for E&T participants. For example, if a class consists of 80% E&T participants, EF is only invoiced for 80% of the instructor’s salary. Before being paid, all costs are vetted using the prudent person principle and determined if said costs are reasonable and necessary for the client's participation in the component. EF POs are also required to search for competitive pricing in the local area when providing supportive payments. This cost parity is verified during the EF oversight process. Outcome Measure Describe the methodology for the outcome reporting measure. Include timeframes being reported (e.g. denominator and numerator) The number and percentage of clients who obtained certification in basic education or foundational skills, or completed a high school equivalency program while enrolled in the component during the FFY. The numerator will include clients who obtained certification in basic education or foundational skills or completed a high school equivalency program from October 1, 2025, to September 30, 2026, during the FFY period. The denominator will include the number of clients that participated in the component during the FFY period, October 1, 2025, to September 30, 2026. 65

body: EDUCATIONAL COMPONENT: CAREER / TECHNICAL EDUCATION PROGRAMS OR OTHER VOCATIONAL TRAINING Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. Placement or continuation in technical education programs or other training opportunities for clients who are enrolled and working towards a degree or industry-recognized certification. This component is also for clients who are enrolled and participating in skills training related to a specific occupation (i.e., welding, word processing, auto mechanics, truck driving school, data entry). Vocational Training may be funded by EF, WIOA, Vocational Rehabilitation, or any other E&T program. Indicate the Target Population this component will serve. Select all that apply. ABAWDs English Language Learners Homeless Individuals who need a HSD or GED Individuals who need a credential Individuals who need a new skill Individuals with disabilities Returning citizens (aka: ex-offenders) Single parents Students Those that reside in rural areas Underemployed Veterans Describe the criteria for participation. Include the skills, knowledge, or experience necessary for participation in the component. For example, literacy or numeracy levels, recent labor market attachment, computer literacy, etc. Clients should have obtained or be on track to obtain a high school diploma or GED before attaching to this component. Clients without the skills, knowledge, and/or prior experience in the occupation of study can meet the criteria for EPC by researching and providing local labor market information to demonstrate how they may benefit from participating in EPC, such as: LMI Gateway analytics print-out O*NET Online employment outlook print-out Local job boards and/or Connecting Colorado Pre-employment offer letter Other documentation that supports the employment opportunity is in-demand or in a growing industry in the local labor market Criteria: Can commit to the level of effort (at least 12 hours a month) Can communicate and produce progress updates for their EF PO upon request Can pursue and obtain employment or enter a WBL or WBL-SUB following completion of the EPC 66

Describe how the State agency ensures that costs attributed to the E&T program are not supplanting funds used for other existing educational programs. All costs are vetted using the "supplement, not supplant" model. Meaning that federal funds cannot be used to perform a service that would normally be paid for with State or local funds. Clients receiving FAFSA are not eligible for tuition assistance from E&T. EF POs check whether clients are receiving FAFSA via CBMS. Clients are allowed to receive assistance with transportation, purchasing books, and other supportive payments based upon assessed need while enrolled in this component. When conducting a comprehensive employability assessment with a client, EF PO workers determine if other funding options can be used for the client’s education costs before E&T funds are used. EF PO workers are required to document this determination through a CBMS case comment and provide appropriate documentation to further demonstrate that E&T funds are not supplanting non-federal funds when appropriate (such as an invoice or document that shows a student’s education is being paid by another funding source) and the State EF team will monitor and review for this evidence during the State’s ongoing oversight process. 67
citation_label: Colorado SNAP E&T State Plan FFY 2026 67
heading: Page 67

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP E&T State Plan FFY 2026

body: Cost parity. Describe how the State agency ensures that costs charged to E&T do not exceed the costs charged for non-E&T participants. EF POs ensure that E&T participants and non-E&T participants are charged the same for services provided by vendors. Costs are prorated by program; EF is only invoiced for E&T participants. For example, if a class consists of 80% E&T participants EF is only invoiced for 80% of the instructor’s salary. Before being paid all costs are vetted using the prudent person principle and determined if said costs are reasonable, allowable, and necessary for the client's participation in the component. EF POs are also required to search for competitive pricing in the local area when providing supportive payments. This cost parity is verified during the State’s ongoing oversight process. Outcome Measure Describe the methodology for the outcome reporting measure. Include timeframes being reported (e.g. denominator and numerator) The number and percentage of clients who obtained certification or successfully completed a career and/or technical education program or other vocational training while enrolled in the component during the FFY. The numerator will include clients who obtained certification or successfully completed a career and/or technical education program or other vocational training from October 1, 2025, to September 30, 2026, during the FFY period. The denominator will include the number of clients that participated in the component during the FFY period, October 1, 2025, to September 30, 2026. 68

body: EDUCATIONAL COMPONENT: WORK READINESS TRAINING Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. EPWRT includes intensive programs that include skill assessment and educational remediation services that prepare individuals for the workforce. Work readiness skills may include both foundational cognitive skills such as reading for information, applied mathematics, locating information, problem solving, and critical thinking and non-cognitive skills, or soft skills, which are defined as personal characteristics and behavioral skills that enhance an individual’s interactions, job performance, and career prospects such as adaptability, integrity, cooperation, and workplace discipline. Clients are required to attend classes online or receive individual instruction on basic/soft skills or skills listed above that increase work readiness. Instruction may also include, but is not limited to, improving communication skills and receiving customer service training. Aztec (online soft skill training and GED preparation) meets the requirements of this activity. Essential Education (an online soft-skill, job search, and GED preparation tool) also meets the requirements of this activity. Indicate the Target Population this component will serve. Select all that apply. ABAWDs English Language Learners Homeless Individuals who need a HSD or GED Individuals who need a credential Individuals who need a new skill Individuals with disabilities Returning citizens (aka: ex-offenders) Single parents Students Those that reside in rural areas Underemployed Veterans Describe the criteria for participation. Include the skills, knowledge, or experience necessary for participation in the component. For example, literacy or numeracy levels, recent labor market attachment, computer literacy, etc. Any EF client that demonstrates and requests a need for basic/soft skills training during the eligibility appointment or during the comprehensive employability assessment or a case management meeting. Individuals who are underemployed and returning citizens are the target populations that are mainly referred to this component. However, any other individual within one of the priority populations who express a need in attaining work readiness training will be referred. Each EF PO will target the populations identified within their EF PO plans and place an individual in this component based on the results of the comprehensive employability assessment and the client’s goals. 72

body: WORK EXPERIENCE COMPONENT: APPRENTICESHIP Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. In Colorado, an apprenticeship is an employer-driven model and type of experiential learning, combining paid, on-the-job learning with related classroom instruction in order to increase an apprentice’s skill level and wages and provide employers with the talent they need to succeed. EF aligns with CDLE’s apprenticeship definition, which ensures that an apprenticeship program is a strong plan that contains all terms and conditions for the qualification, recruitment, selection, employment, and training of apprentices, including the requirements for a written apprenticeship agreement between the apprentice and the sponsor. Apprenticeships can be provided by a community college, technical school, apprenticeship training school, or by the employer at multiple locations. EF POs will partner with training providers, apprenticeship providers, and employers in their area to develop internal business processes that identify when clients are ready for WBLA. Apprenticeships must be a sustained interaction with industry or community professionals in real-world settings to the extent practicable or simulated environments at an educational institution that foster in-depth, firsthand engagement with the tasks required in a given career field, that are aligned to curriculum and instruction. Apprenticeships emphasize employer engagement, include specific training objectives, and lead to regular employment. While engaged in this component, clients have access to educational and career counseling and other supportive services, directly or indirectly, as well as opportunities to attain at least one industry-recognized credential. All work activities and unsubsidized employment placements are subject to the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), and minimum wage laws apply. Is this component subsidized by SNAP E&T? Subsidized Unsubsidized Both subsidized and unsubsidized Indicate the Target Population this component will serve. Select all that apply. ABAWDs English Language Learners Homeless Individuals who need a HSD or GED Individuals who need a credential Individuals who need a new skill Individuals with disabilities Returning citizens (aka: ex-offenders) Single parents Students Those that reside in rural areas Underemployed Veterans 75

body: WORK EXPERIENCE COMPONENT: INTERNSHIP Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. WBLI is a form of experiential learning that integrates knowledge and theory learned in the classroom with practical application and skill development in a professional setting. WBLI gives students the opportunity to gain valuable applied experience and make connections in professional fields they are considering for career paths. WBLI also gives employers an opportunity to guide and evaluate an EF client’s performance. WBLI will be a short-term (up to 12 months), hands-on, supervised work experience with a professional organization that is designed to increase a student's knowledge of a professional career field. WBLIs range from 20 - 400 hours total and include intentional learning objectives related to increasing student knowledge, training to develop additional skills, and quality supervision to guide and mentor the student. All work activities and unsubsidized employment placements are subject to the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), and minimum wage laws apply. Employers, EF POs, and training providers will collaborate to develop curriculum for clients in WBLI to provide the student the opportunity to apply the knowledge gained in the classroom in a work setting. This curriculum will clearly define learning objectives/goals related to the professional goals of the student’s academic coursework. It must not be simply to advance the operations of the employer or be the work that a regular employee would routinely perform. Labor market research and the skills needed in WBLI will be used to identify if the skills or knowledge learned are transferable to other employment settings. Employers, EF POs, and training providers will define a beginning and end and develop a job description with desired qualifications based on labor market reach and the skills/qualifications needed to obtain employment following the WBLI. WBLIs can last up to 12 months. The employer must provide supervision by a professional with expertise and an educational and/or professional background in the field of the experience. The experienced supervisor must also provide routine feedback. The employer must provide resources, equipment, and facilities (identified in labor market research, such as job postings, etc.) that support learning objectives/goals. A WBLI should be in an in-demand career within the local area and can be demonstrated through: LMI Gateway analytics print-out O*NET Online employment outlook print-out Local job boards and/or Connecting Colorado Pre-employment offer letter Other documentation that supports the employment opportunity is in-demand or in a growing industry in the local labor market Is this component subsidized by SNAP E&T? Subsidized Unsubsidized Both subsidized and unsubsidized 78

body: WORK EXPERIENCE COMPONENT: ON-THE-JOB TRAINING Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. Colorado’s EF program aligns with CDLE’s WIOA definition of WBLOJT. WBLOJT is training by an employer that is provided to a paid participant while engaged in productive work in a job that: 1. Provides knowledge or skills essential to the full and adequate performance of the job; 2. Is made available through a program that provides reimbursement to the employer of up to 100% (varies by EF PO) of the wage rate of the client for the extraordinary costs of providing the training and additional supervision related to the training; and 3. Is limited in duration as appropriate to the occupation for which the client is being trained, taking into account the content of the training, the prior work experience of the client, and the service strategy of the client, as appropriate. EF POs may place clients in a WBLOJT through a contract with an employer or apprenticeship program sponsor in the public, private non-profit, or private sector. A WBLOJT contract must be limited to the period of time required for a participant to become proficient in the occupation for which the training is being provided (ranging from 1 - 6 months). In determining the appropriate length of the contract, consideration should be given to the skill requirements of the occupation, the academic and occupational skill level of the participant, prior work experience, and the participant's individual employment plan (20 CFR 680.700). WBLOJT is defined as paid training by an employer that is provided to an EF client while engaged in productive work in a job that: Provides the knowledge or skills essential to the full and adequate performance of the job; Is limited in duration as appropriate to the occupation for which the EF client is participating in the OJT, taking into account the content of the training, the prior work experience of the client, and the service strategy of the client, as appropriate. Although EF WBLOJT is defined by WIOA, WIOA OJT is not included in this component. All work activities and unsubsidized employment placements are subject to the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), and minimum wage laws apply. Indicate the Target Population this component will serve. Select all that apply. ABAWDs English Language Learners Homeless Individuals who need a HSD or GED Individuals who need a credential Individuals who need a new skill Individuals with disabilities Returning citizens (aka: ex-offenders) Single parents Students Those that reside in rural areas Underemployed Veterans 81

body: WORK EXPERIENCE COMPONENT: PRE-APPRENTICESHIP Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. In Colorado, a pre-apprenticeship program prepares clients to enter and succeed in a registered apprenticeship program. EF adopts CDLE’s definition of pre-apprenticeship, which includes: (1) Have a clearly articulated relationship to one or more registered apprenticeships (2) Have an opportunity to enroll their graduates into a registered apprenticeship. In addition, Pre-apprenticeships must assist clients by helping them meet entry requirements (e.g. GED, assessment, certification), exposing them to an unfamiliar occupation, and/or building soft skills. Instruction can be provided by a community college, technical school, apprenticeship training school, or by the employer. EF POs will partner with training providers, apprenticeship providers, and employers in their area to develop internal business processes that identifies when clients are ready for WBLPA. Pre-apprenticeships range from 2-3 months with a minimum of 100 to a maximum of 400 hours per pre apprenticeship opportunity. Education partners collaborate with employers to develop curricula based on skills and knowledge needed by the apprentice. The instruction can be provided at the school, online, or at the work site. Pre-apprenticeship are sustained interaction with industry or community professionals in real-world settings to the extent practicable or simulated environments at an educational institution that foster in-depth, firsthand engagement with the tasks required in a given career field, that are aligned to curriculum and instruction. Pre-apprenticeships and apprenticeships emphasize employer engagement, include specific training objectives, and lead to regular employment. Clients are screened for this component during the initial comprehensive employability asssessment and/or following completion of an educational program. Clients have access to educational and career counseling and other supportive payments, directly or indirectly while engaged in this component, as well as opportunities to attain at least one industry-recognized credential. All work activities and unsubsidized employment placements are subject to the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) and minimum wage laws apply. Is this component subsidized by SNAP E&T? Subsidized Unsubsidized Both subsidized and unsubsidized 84

body: WORK EXPERIENCE COMPONENT: TRANSITIONAL JOBS Description of the component. Provide a summary of the activities and services. WBLTJ consists of jobs in the public, private, or nonprofit sectors and are only available for individuals with barriers to employment who are chronically unemployed or who have an inconsistent work history. Transitional jobs provide individuals with work experience and an opportunity to develop workplace skills within the context of an employee-employer relationship. In a WBLTJ that is unsubsidized, the employer will act as the Employer of Record and pay the client’s wage. The EF PO and employer collaborate to develop a curriculum and duration for the transitional job on a case-by-case basis, based on the client’s need, local labor market information, and the occupation the WBLTJ is in. The curriculum will be tailored to the training activities and work assignments needed for the client to develop the skills necessary to attach to long-term sustainable employment once the transitional job has ended. A transitional job activity/curriculum may include: Integration of training with work (e.g., Safeserv certification in the food preparation industry) Safety and general position duty training Work schedule and time sheet responsibilities Training/skill attainment milestones specific to the occupation (e.g., number of hours needed to earn a fork-lift operation certificate) Frequent performance reviews and/or pre- and post- skill assessments. Clients should not participate in a transitional job opportunity for more than 12 months. The minimum level of effort is 12 hours a month in this component. The types of employers and jobs that this component will target is based on the in-demand occupations within a county or region. All work activities and unsubsidized employment placements are subject to the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) and minimum wage laws apply. Is this component subsidized by SNAP E&T? Subsidized Unsubsidized Both subsidized and unsubsidized Indicate the Target Population this component will serve. Select all that apply. ABAWDs English Language Learners Homeless Individuals who need a HSD or GED Individuals who need a credential Individuals who need a new skill Individuals with disabilities Returning citizens (aka: ex-offenders) Single parents Students Those that reside in rural areas Underemployed Veterans 87

body: Describe the criteria for participation. Include the skills, knowledge, or experience necessary for participation in the component. For example, literacy or numeracy levels, recent labor market attachment, computer literacy, etc. Individuals who meet the below qualifying criteria and have the ability to address barriers to employment (demonstrated through the comprehensive employability assessment) may attach to WBLTJ. Criteria: Have an HSD or GED, or are able to participate in a ABE, HSD, or GED program during their participation in a WBLTJ. Can commit to the level of effort (at least 12 hours a month) Can work with an EF PO to address barriers to employment (such as receiving legal services, attending work readiness classes, and other services that assist the client with finding employment immediately following completion of a WBLTJ). When required, can answer pre- and post-skill assessments Can commute to and from the WBLTJ employer’s host site Can communicate and produce progress updates for their EF PO upon request Can pursue and obtain employment following completion of the WBLTJ Chronically underemployed or have an inconsistent work history A WBLTJ should be in an in-demand career within the local area and can be demonstrated through: LMI Gateway analytics print-out O*NET Online employment outlook print-out Local job boards and/or Connecting Colorado Pre-employment offer letter Other documentation that supports the employment opportunity is in-demand or in a growing industry in the local labor market Indicate the geographic areas where this component is offered. body: Provide a brief explanation that describes how the provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment. The provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment by implementing a structured, client-centered career training pipeline through the Employment First Program. In partnership with service providers, the program connects participants to targeted training opportunities via a dedicated liaison. The model uses individualized training plans based on comprehensive assessments to guide participants through soft skills development, technical training aligned with industry needs, and job readiness support. body: Provide a brief explanation that describes how the provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment. Throughout the pre-apprenticeship, participants build connections to potential employers through fieldwork, instruction, and site visits conducted by employer partners. The Workforce Development manager and Case Manager work together on resume building, interview skills, and job readiness training. They connect pre-apprentices to available job opportunities, provide direct support in the application process, and serve as a reference during the interview process. Post-employment, they continue to check in with employers and employees to identify issues and break down barriers before they become too big. Through internships with The Park People, former TreeForce participants are placed with external organizations for a 3-6 month period. During this time, they join a work crew to learn the basics of tree care on the job. At the conclusion of the internship, interns may be hired on by the organization, or a direct connection to an employer is made. These internships provide an accelerated pathway to private sector employment, giving them a head start in their first full-time position. Does the work site employer or other industry employer partners hire the majority of the activity graduates? body: Provide a brief explanation that describes how the provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment. The provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment by implementing a structured, client-centered career training pipeline through the Employment First Program. In partnership with service providers, the program connects participants to targeted training opportunities via a dedicated liaison. The model—successfully in place since 2021—uses individualized training plans based on comprehensive assessments to guide participants through soft skills development, technical training aligned with industry needs, and job readiness support. Training is delivered through workshops, one-on-one coaching, and partner-led sessions, with regular assessments to monitor progress and ensure alignment with employer expectations. body: Provide a brief explanation that describes how the provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment. At the end of the program, and upon completion of their coursework, participants spend time at a district host site completing the WBL portion of the program. At the end of their WBL, the participant is given an opportunity to indicate their interest in an open position. The district will then set up an interview for the participant. Once hired by the district, the participant is eligible to pursue the Apprenticeship program when they are ready to continue developing in their career. Job Retention Services, including career coaching and support services, will also be available to support SNAP participants as they move from completing the Pre-Apprenticeship into employment at the district. While we intend for participants to move into employment at their district upon completing the program, Job Retention Services will also be available to SNAP participants who may gain employment elsewhere upon completion of the program. body: Provide a brief explanation that describes how the provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment. Throughout the pre-apprenticeship, participants build connections to potential employers through fieldwork, instruction, and site visits conducted by employer partners. The Workforce Development manager and Case Manager work together on resume building, interview skills, and job readiness training. They connect pre-apprentices to available job opportunities, provide direct support in the application process, and serve as a reference during the interview process. Post-employment, they continue to check in with employers and employees to identify issues and break down barriers before they become too big. Through internships with The Park People, former TreeForce participants are placed with external organizations for a 3-6 month period. During this time, they join a work crew to learn the basics of tree care on the job. At the conclusion of the internship, interns may be hired on by the organization, or a direct connection to an employer is made. These internships provide an accelerated pathway to private sector employment, giving them a head start in their first full-time position. body: Provide a brief explanation that describes how the provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment. The provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment by implementing a structured, client-centered career training pipeline through the Employment First Program. In partnership with service providers, the program connects participants to targeted training opportunities via a dedicated liaison. The model uses individualized training plans based on comprehensive assessments to guide participants through soft skills development, technical training aligned with industry needs, and job readiness support. Training is delivered through workshops, one-on-one coaching, and partner-led sessions, with regular assessments to monitor progress and ensure alignment with employer expectations. Does the work site employer or other industry employer partners hire the majority of the activity graduates? body: Provide a brief explanation that describes how the provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment. The provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment by implementing a structured, client-centered career training pipeline through the Employment First Program. In partnership with service providers, the program connects participants to targeted training opportunities via a dedicated liaison. The model uses individualized training plans based on comprehensive assessments to guide participants through soft skills development, technical training aligned with industry needs, and job readiness support. Training is delivered through workshops, one-on-one coaching, and partner-led sessions, with regular assessments to monitor progress and ensure alignment with employer expectations. The provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment by implementing a structured, client-centered career training pipeline through the Employment First Program. In partnership with service providers, the program connects participants to targeted training opportunities via a dedicated liaison. The model—successfully in place since 2021—uses individualized training plans based on comprehensive assessments to guide participants through soft skills development, technical training aligned with industry needs, and job readiness support. Training is delivered through workshops, one-on-one coaching, and partner-led sessions, with regular assessments to monitor progress and ensure alignment with employer expectations. The provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment by implementing a structured, client-centered career training pipeline through the Employment First Program. In partnership with service providers, the program connects participants to targeted training opportunities via a dedicated liaison. The model uses individualized training plans based on comprehensive assessments to guide participants through soft skills development, technical training aligned with industry needs, and job readiness support. Training is delivered through workshops, one-on-one coaching, and partner-led sessions, with regular assessments to monitor progress and ensure alignment with employer expectations. body: Provide a brief explanation that describes how the provider focuses on moving participants promptly into regular, unsubsidized employment. Pre-apprenticeship students in their third week meet with our Employment Specialist to begin the employment process. That includes updating their resumes, interview practice, and creating a list of potential employers. In the next five weeks, they will research employers and fill out applications while completing their culinary training. Their Employment Specialist will continue to oversee and support them. body: OPERATING BUDGET The regulations at 7 CFR 273.7(c)(6) outline State agencies must include an operating budget for the year. Complete all cost categories, as applicable. Note that the cost categories, outside of the contractual or county administered program line items, apply only to the State agency costs. The calculated values will automatically display upon selecting the Quick Save button. Direct Program and Admin Costs Non-Federal Share Federal Share Total Salary/Wages (State agency only) $247,513.00 $539,185.00 $786,698.00 List Full Time Equivalent (FTE) staff positions, percentage of time spent on the project, and average annual salary of each position. Example: E&T Manager - $60,000 * .50 FTE = $30,000; 5 E&T Counselors = $25,000 * 1.00 FTEs * 5 = $125,000. Administrator of Strategic Partnerships, 0.60 FTE X $115,000.00 = $69,000.00 Policy and Oversight Supervisor , 0.75 FTE X $95,000.00 = $71,250.00 Policy & Oversight Program Lead, 0.75 FTE X $80,000.00 = $60,000.00 Policy & Oversight Coordinators, 1.50 FTE X $130,000.00 =$195,000.00 Partner Management Supervisor, 0.60 FTE X $95,000.00 = $57,000.00 Partner Management Program Lead, 0.80 FTE X $80,000.00 = $64,000.00 Partner Management Liaison, 1.50 FTE X $70,000.00 = $105,000.00 Resource Coordinator 0.60 FTE X $70,000.00 = $42,000.00 Contracts and Procurement Supervisor, 0.33 FTE X $95,000.00 = $31,350.00 Contracts and Procurement Lead, 0.75 FTE X $80,000.00 = $60,000.00 Program Evaluator, 1.00 FTE X $90,000.00 = $90,000.00 Senior Fiscal and Policy Advisor, 1.00 FTE X $90,000.00 = $90,000.00 Pro-rata costs to Director and Executive 0.33 FTE X $125,000 = $41,250.00 staff and Department support staff) Total 10.51 FTE X $1,215,000 = 975,850 The amounts listed above include annual salaries and fringe benefits. Non-Federal Share Federal Share Total Fringe Benefits $54,478.00 $118,675.00 $173,153.00 Explain how fringe benefits are calculated and clearly explain how the amount listed was determined. If charging fringe benefits to the E&T program, provide the approved fringe rates. Fringe benefits are calculated as a percentage of total salary and wages and include costs associated with group insurance (e.g., health, dental, life), retirement contributions, and other employee benefits provided by the Colorado Department of Human Services. The fringe benefit rate used for this plan is 22.1% and is based on actual expenditures for FFY26. 417

body: Non-Federal Share Federal Share Total Non-Capital Equipment $0.00 $2,000.00 $2,000.00 Describe non-capital equipment and supplies to be purchased with E&T funds. An estimated $2,000 for computer equipment and other office equipment (printer/scanners) Non-Federal Share Federal Share Total Materials $0.00 $2,000.00 $2,000.00 Describe materials to be purchased with E&T funds. Materials, including marketing materials, office supplies, and technical equipment other than computer leases. Non-Federal Share Federal Share Total Travel $0.00 $12,000.00 $12,000.00 Describe the purpose and frequency of staff travel charged to the E&T program. This should not include E&T participant reimbursements for transportation. Include planned staff training and registration costs for training that will be charged to E&T funds. Staff travel costs charged to the E&T program support the administration, oversight, and continuous improvement of SNAP E&T operations at the state level. Travel is primarily conducted to provide technical assistance to local providers, conduct monitoring and management evaluations, and participate in SNAP E&T-related meetings, conferences, and workforce development events. These activities ensure alignment with federal program requirements, promote consistency across partners, and support the overall effectiveness of the program. Travel is expected to occur periodically throughout the year, at least once a quarter, with State E&T staff participating in multiple in-state engagements based on programmatic needs, local site support, and annual oversight schedules. All staff travel and training expenses charged to the E&T program are necessary, reasonable, and directly related to the administration and improvement of the SNAP E&T program, in compliance with federal cost principles. body: FUNDING SOURCES Fields for the Funding Sources section will populate from other sections, such as the Operating Budget section or annual allocations decided by FNS OET. The system will provide the States 100 percent allocation as well as the target for the total 50/50 funds, as provided in the annual E&T final allocation memo. State agencies may enter funds into the field "100 Percent Federal Grant - Additional Funds" for planning purposes. This field must be blank before initial submission. State agencies that wish to request additional 100% funds can do so via the Funding Requests tab. If the request is approved, State agencies will see the approved amount populated in this field, and a new State Plan Amendment must be submitted. The system utilizes a formula that distributes administrative costs to the various funding sources (i.e. 100 percent Federal, 50 percent Federal Admin and 50 percent Non-Federal Admin.) The formula also establishes a funding hierarchy for the use of all available 100 percent Federal funds. This funding hierarchy will assign the planned administrative expenses against the regular 100 Federal grant first, then depending upon availability, against additional 100 percent funds, able-bodied adults without dependents (ABAWD) pledge funds, if applicable. Any planned costs over the available 100 percent funds will be evenly distributed against the 50 percent Admin funds. The planned expenses shown for the field "100% Federal Grant" will be inclusive of the formula allocation, as well as any additional Federal funds approved. Fields in the column “Distribution of Planned Expenses” are populated from the planned expenses table. States can use this table to extrapolate figures, but cannot submit the form until 100% of Federal additional funds under the "Allocation or Target" column has been removed. 422

body: PLEDGE TO SERVE ALL AT-RISK ABAWDS The Act authorizes FNS to allocate $20 million annually to State agencies that commit, or pledge, to ensuring the availability of education, training, or workfare opportunities that permit able-bodied adults without dependents (ABAWDs) to remain eligible beyond the 3-month time limit. To be eligible for these additional funds (pledge funds), State agencies must pledge to offer and provide an opportunity in a work program that meets the participation requirements of 7 CFR 273.24 to every applicant and recipient who is in the last month of the 3-month time limit and not otherwise exempt. Individuals are exempt from the time limit if they meet an exception under 7 CFR 273.24(c), reside in an area covered by a waiver in accordance with 7 CFR 273.24(f), or who are exempted by the State under 7 CFR 273.24(g). ABAWDs who meet the criteria outlined in 7 CFR 273.7(d)(3)(i) are referred to as “at-risk” ABAWDs. Is the State agency pledging to offer qualifying activities to all at-risk ABAWDs? Yes No 425
