citation_label: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan
heading: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan

metadata.title: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan

body: Colorado’s Section 20 Optional Workfare Program State Plan for FFY26 Date of Submission: 8/15/2025
citation_label: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan 1
heading: Page 1

metadata.title: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan

body:
Colorado plans to operate a Section 20 Optional Workfare program in Federal Fiscal Year 2026 (FFY26) for work registrants. County Departments of Human Services (DHS) that operate the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program’s Employment and Training (SNAP E&T) program, known as Employment First (EF) in Colorado, have the option to operate a Section 20 Optional Workfare Program. At the time of this plan submission, the Section 20 Optional Workfare Program will operate in 16 of Colorado’s 64 counties for FFY26.

Colorado’s philosophy is that workfare provides a stepping stone to paid employment while providing valuable service to the community. Through a robust network of workfare sites, Colorado can connect clients with a wide array of employers where they can build relevant skills and gain valuable work experience. In FFY26, Colorado will continue to align, where possible, workfare sites with Colorado’s key industries to meet local business needs and ensure that the skill development directly ties to the interest and aptitude of each client and the local labor market.

Colorado's key industries include:

Healthcare and Social Assistance Administrative Support and Waste Management Remediation Services Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services Retail Trade Manufacturing Accommodation and Food Service Educational Services Finance and Insurance Information Construction Wholesale Trade In FFY26, the Section 20 Optional Workfare Program will operate as a voluntary program. As a voluntary program, any eligible work registrant can access program services. Work registrants will not be sanctioned for electing not to participate in the Section 20 Optional Workfare Program. The table below lists each county DHS with a Section 20 Optional Workfare Program, and the number of workfare sites in the county.

Adams (8) Arapahoe (44) Boulder (5) Broomfield (4) Weld (5) Denver (24) Douglas (36) Eagle (2) Garfield (13) Jefferson (22) Larimer (2) Logan (18) Prowers (7) Chaffee (1) Montrose (11) Morgan (19)

Colorado has 234 workfare sites statewide. County DHS continues to identify, develop, and contract with value-added workforce sites. The local workfare coordinator in each County DHS department develops its own Section 20 Optional Workfare sites. The EF program tracks the workfare sites each County DHS utilizes, averaging roughly 14 workfare sites per county. 1
citation_label: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan 2
heading: Page 2

metadata.title: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan

body:
These workfare sites are located in a variety of public and nonprofit agencies which include but are not limited to: County DHS, Colorado Department of Transportation, Habitat for Humanity ReStores, Habitat for Humanity, public schools, museums, daycare centers, homeless projects, hospitals, homeless shelters, libraries, senior centers, Native American centers, historic sites, public radio stations, community colleges, humane societies, food and clothing banks, workforce centers, parks and recreation departments, road & bridge departments, non-profit organizations such as Goodwill, Arc Thrift Stores, the Salvation Army, the Red Cross, Meals on Wheels, women’s shelters, and counseling centers.

Some examples of Section 20 Optional Workfare job classifications are: data entry clerk, clerical/receptionist, medical/nursing assistant, computer operator, building inspector, daycare worker, landfill worker, cemetery staff, teacher’s aide, peer counselor, job coach, gardener, food service worker, van driver, general laborer, maintenance, cashier, painter, customer service representative, carpenter, tutor for English as a second language, bookkeeper, marketing clerk, dietary aide, counselor, cook, inmate intake/release monitor, interpreter, forest service worker, construction worker, mailroom clerk, landscaper, historical guide, animal caretaker, retail clerk, senior aide, public radio station staff, researcher, librarian aide, telephone operator, bus aide, warehouse worker, and hospice worker.

A program orientation, assessment, and an individualized service plan are completed by the workfare coordinator or an EF case manager before and throughout the workfare site placement. The assessment includes, but is not limited to: strengths, interests, and or/ability tests, screening for employment barriers, evaluation of work history, and education. During their participation in workfare, each client is encouraged to work on three job search competencies, including the completion of a master job application/resume, formulation of a detailed job search plan, and development of job interviewing skills. Both the client and the workfare coordinator agree upon the workfare assignment. Concerted efforts are made to begin workfare at the initial EF program appointment. Every attempt is made to make a successful match based on the abilities and interests of each client while targeting training in a convenient location. Workfare coordinators provide written job descriptions, skills to be learned, and hours of operation for each workfare site so clients can choose which workfare site best meets the client’s needs and aligns with career interests. In many cases, clients may engage in a 30-day supervised job search prior to or following their application or recertification appointment for SNAP benefits (dependent upon the needs of the client). Clients are then assessed within 14 days from the date of the referral by an EF case manager. After this period, the client may be assigned to a workfare site. Before placement, the workfare coordinator visits the workfare site to ensure that a safe and positive working environment exists. A written agreement detailing the responsibilities of both the workfare site sponsor and the workfare coordinator is signed before a client is placed in the workfare site. The written agreements shall be revisited at least every two years to ensure the workfare site continues to add value to the Section 20 Optional Workfare Program and is suitable for clients. To be suitable, a workfare site must meet the following criteria: ● not pose an unreasonable risk to health or safety ● be within the physical and mental capabilities of the client ● be within one hour's distance from the client’s residence ● not interfere with a client’s religious observances 2
citation_label: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan 3
heading: Page 3

metadata.title: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan

body:
● not be subject to a strike or lockout For all workfare sites, clients receive a written notification that contains: ● Where and when to report ● The workfare site supervisor’s name and phone number ● The job description ● The number of required hours ● The penalty for program non-compliance ● notification of eligibility ● The client’s specific responsibilities ● Information on reimbursement for travel and other participation-related costs (that are covered through Section 20 Optional Workfare Participant reimbursements). Required monthly workfare hours are determined by dividing the entire SNAP household grant by the prevailing Colorado minimum or federal minimum wage, whichever is higher. This year, the Colorado minimum wage is higher. Fractions of hours are always rounded down. The required hours cannot exceed eight hours per day or 30 hours per week. Workers’ compensation is provided at all sites either by the individual workfare site or by the county DHS where the program operates.

Workfare coordinators monitor attendance and progress for all clients at least monthly. Interactive monthly follow-up groups can be offered as part of the Section 20 Optional Workfare Program. Follow-up groups can include client networking, a continuation of job-seeking skills, guest speakers, including employers that are currently hiring, and peer support and encouragement. Workfare site timesheets may also be collected at the monthly meetings. Client reimbursement for transportation or other needs that are reasonable and necessary (e.g., tools, clothing, fees) should be disbursed based on the client’s assessed needs.

The timesheets include an evaluation form completed by the site director/supervisor, and case notes are created by the case managers and kept in the client’s case file. The combined timesheet/evaluation form serves as client feedback and assists workfare coordinators in promptly addressing placement concerns. Clients use this feedback when job searching to show potential employers their current work habits. Often, contacts made at workfare sites are used as job references for future employment opportunities. Any workers’ compensation deductibles incurred by the county department of human/social services because of a client receiving a workplace injury during participation in workfare shall fall under the 50/50 administrative funding rules. Lastly, county DHSs will support clients by assigning them to workfare sites in another county if the latter site better fits their needs than the one in their respective county.

3
citation_label: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan 4
heading: Page 4

metadata.title: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan

body:
Workfare Site Sponsor Responsibilities ● Provide an orientation or 1:1 appointment concerning workfare site rules and regulations. ● Provide an assignment that the client is capable of and interested in performing. ● Provide positive supervision and the necessary equipment to complete the assignment. ● Complete the timesheet/evaluation forms for each client every month when the client is engaged in the workfare site. ● Provide time off for a client’s job interviews. ● Communicate any relevant issues, either concerning the client, the job application, or the general operation of the workfare site, to the workfare coordinator.

Client Responsibilities ● Observe the working hours and the work rules established by the workfare site. ● Communicate with the workfare site supervisor, workfare coordinator, or the EF case manager regarding workfare site progress or concerns. ● Notify the workfare coordinator or EF case manager immediately if the client stops participating in their workfare site placement. ● Turn in completed timesheets or other required paperwork digitally or in person at follow-up appointments.

Disqualification

ABAWDs are not subject to disqualifications for not participating in the Section 20 Optional Workfare Program at the time of this plan’s submission.

Record Keeping

Written workfare site agreements are executed with all agencies and organizations that serve as workfare sites. These agreements are kept in a master file at each local agency (the county DHS or subcontractor) that operates the Section 20 Optional Workfare Program. In addition, county DHS or their sub-contractors must furnish the State with the names, addresses, and hours of specific clinics and doctors that will treat injured clients for workers’ compensation purposes. This information is required for each workfare site. All workfare site agreements are reviewed, and a copy is retained by the state office. These agreements are updated when changes occur and at least every two years. Case files, both electronic and hard copy, are maintained for each client. Case files include workfare sites, hours assigned, hours actually worked, timesheet/evaluation forms, and any communication with SNAP eligibility staff, workfare site sponsors, and the client. Case files that include an individual service plan, assessments, case notes, and other program-supporting documentation are maintained for audit and review purposes for at least three years from the month of origin for each Section 20 Optional Workfare program case. Clients are seen at least monthly to review their progress at their workfare site. Each month, the client or the workfare site supervisor submits a timesheet/evaluation form to the workfare coordinator.

4
citation_label: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan 5
heading: Page 5

metadata.title: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan

body:
Reporting Quarterly reports are available to the Mountains Plains Regional Office (MPRO) with the Food and Nutrition Service (FNS) office within 45 days after the end of each quarter. Reports are detailed by each county department of human/social services that operates a Section 20 Optional Workfare Program: ● Number of households referred, ● Number of households assigned to workfare sites, ● Number of individual clients assigned to workfare sites, ● The number of total hours worked by clients at workfare sites, ● Number of disqualifications due to workfare non-compliance (not applicable at the time of this plan submission), ● The date a client's SNAP allotment changed ● The amount of a client's SNAP allotment changed ● Employment wage ● The estimated amount of enhanced funding Enhanced funding, also known as the workfare savings entitlement, occurs when a client begins unsubsidized employment after participating in workfare for the first time and within 30 days of ending their first participation in workfare. Quarterly reports also supply details on each client who generated enhanced funding.

Program Evaluation Section 20 Optional Workfare Program agencies (county departments of human/social services and their sub-contractors) are monitored on an ongoing basis (at least once every two years) by State EF staff. This monitoring can include, but is not limited to: ● Virtual or on-site evaluations ● Interviews with local workfare coordinators ● Case file reviews ● Observation of orientations and assessments ● Evaluation of management practices ● Review of participation and enhanced funding reports ● Visits to workfare sites (if possible) ● Review of budget and program goals ● Examination of local operations to ensure compliance with state and federal regulations. The State EF team provides technical assistance to Section 20 Optional Workfare Program agencies throughout the year. Last year (FFY25), Colorado produced updated workfare guidance, including documented procedures and forms. These serve as the State EF team’s platform to communicate and deliver training regarding EF and the Section 20 Optional Workfare Program, including discussions regarding best practices, policies, and procedures. The State of Colorado will also host monthly workgroups in FFY26 to support program operations. Hearing Procedure

5
citation_label: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan 6
heading: Page 6

metadata.title: Colorado Workfare Program State Plan

body: Each individual or household has the right to a fair hearing to appeal a denial, reduction, or termination of benefits due to a determination of non-exempt status or failure to comply with the work registration requirement of workfare. If a fair hearing is requested, the household shall be allowed to examine its workfare case file before the hearing. Information not released to the household may not be used at the hearing. The results of the hearing shall be binding for the SNAP office. 6

heading: Page 7

citation_label: Energy EBT
heading: Energy EBT

metadata.title: Energy EBT

body: The Colorado Energy EBT program provides one-time cash assistance for energy costs to certain households that receive Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) benefits.
citation_label: Energy EBT 1
heading: Energy EBT

metadata.title: Energy EBT

body:
If you need assistance with your Energy EBT benefit, contact the Energy EBT program by phone at 800.536.5298 or by email at CDHS_energyebt@state.co.us .

For more information on how to use your EBT card, visit Colorado EBT .

For questions about your SNAP case, contact your county human services department .
citation_label: Energy EBT 2
heading: Contact information

metadata.title: Energy EBT

body:
Energy EBT is a state program that provides one-time cash assistance for energy costs to SNAP households that have not already received benefits from the Low-income Energy Assistance Program (LEAP) in the past 12 months and who have a household member who is aged 60 or older or has a disability.

SNAP households that receive Energy EBT will be assigned the highest standard utility allowance in their SNAP budget, which may result in an increase to their monthly SNAP allotment.
citation_label: Energy EBT 3
heading: What is Energy EBT?

metadata.title: Energy EBT

body:
A SNAP household is automatically eligible for Energy EBT if:

The household is eligible for SNAP and is currently receiving SNAP benefits; and

The household has a member who is aged 60 or older or has a disability; and

The household has not received LEAP at any address in the previous 12 months; and

The household has not received Energy EBT at any address in the previous 12 months.
citation_label: Energy EBT 4
heading: Who is eligible for Energy EBT?

metadata.title: Energy EBT

body: The benefit is a single cash payment of $21 per eligible SNAP household.
citation_label: Energy EBT 5
heading: How much is the Energy EBT benefit?

metadata.title: Energy EBT

body:
New SNAP applications:

Your Energy EBT benefit will be available at the same time as your first SNAP benefit.

SNAP recertifications and periodic reports:

Your Energy EBT benefit will be available by the time of your next SNAP benefit.
citation_label: Energy EBT 6
heading: When will I receive my Energy EBT benefit?

metadata.title: Energy EBT

body: No. SNAP households will automatically receive the Energy EBT benefit if they are determined to be eligible through the above criteria.
citation_label: Energy EBT 7
heading: Do I need to apply for Energy EBT?

metadata.title: Energy EBT

body:
Question Energy EBT SNAP What type of assistance is it? Cash Food How frequently is it issued? One time Monthly Do I have to apply? No Yes Can I withdraw the benefit as cash from an ATM? Yes No

Eligibility for Energy EBT is automatically determined at the time of a household's SNAP application, recertification or periodic report.

If you do not already receive another cash assistance program, such as Colorado Works or Old Age Pension:

The Energy EBT benefit will be issued onto your existing EBT card where your SNAP benefits are issued. If you cannot find your EBT card, call EBT Customer Service at 888.328.2656 for a replacement.

If you already receive another cash assistance program, such as Colorado Works or Old Age Pension:

The Energy EBT benefit will be issued in the same way as the other cash assistance. This could be by EBT card, direct deposit or a prepaid debit card.

Energy EBT can be used at any authorized retailer to make a purchase, to get cash back with a purchase, or to withdraw funds from an ATM with the EBT logo.

Please note:

There is a 32-cent fee each time you withdraw cash from an ATM; any additional ATM surcharge may apply.

Some retailers may allow cash withdrawals at their customer service desk.

EBT cards cannot be used at at any of the following prohibited locations:

Places licensed to sell malt, vinous or spirited liquors, including retail liquor stores, brewpubs and bars;

Places where gambling activities occur, such as casinos, racetracks or bingo halls;

Places that sell medical or retail marijuana or marijuana products;

Places that offer adult-oriented entertainment, including adult video stores, strip clubs and peep shows; and

Places whose primary business is the sale of firearms.

If a SNAP household meets the Energy EBT eligibility criteria at the time of their recertification or periodic report, then the household will receive the benefit at that time. A SNAP household cannot receive Energy EBT more than one time in a 12-month period.

Any Energy EBT benefits that are unused 274 days after issuance will be removed from your EBT account.

The Energy EBT benefit amount is a standard $21 for eligible SNAP households.

No. Energy EBT is cash assistance intended to help offset a household’s energy costs; however, Colorado does not direct how the cash assistance is used, except for that it cannot be used at prohibited locations (liquor and marijuana stores, casinos, etc.).

Yes. LEAP accepts applications between Nov. 1 and April 30. Visit LEAP for more information.

No.

citation_label: OM-FEA-2025-0002 Energy EBT Implementation
heading: OM-FEA-2025-0002 Energy EBT Implementation

metadata.title: OM-FEA-2025-0002 Energy EBT Implementation

body:
1575 Sherman Street, Denver, CO 80203 P 303.866.5700 cdhs.colorado.gov Jared Polis, Governor | Michelle Barnes, Executive Director

OPERATION MEMO

Title: Energy EBT Implementation Office/Division: Office of Economic Security, Food and Energy Assistance Division Memo number: OM-FEA-2025-0002 Program area: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP), Energy EBT (E-EBT) Outcome: County human service departments are aware of the new SNAP Energy EBT program and its implementation date. Pertinent statute/rule: 9 CCR 2503-10; CRS § 26-2-307 Keywords: SNAP, Energy-EBT, LEAP, EBT Issue date: February 27, 2025 Effective: February 8, 2025 Expires: December 31, 2025

Intended recipients This communication will be sent to all directors of county human service departments. Directors shall forward pertinent information to staff members.

Purpose The purpose of this memo is to ensure that county human service departments are aware of the new SNAP Energy EBT (E-EBT) program and its implementation date. The related CBMS updates were released with the February 8, 2025 build, with the vast majority of eligible SNAP households set to receive the new E-EBT benefit in early March 2025. Action Upon receipt of this memo, county leadership should share the information with all program staff and encourage them to review the CBMS Build Project February 2025 training materials through the Staff Development Division (SDD). Background CDHS is required to implement a program to distribute fuel assistance payments on and after July 1, 2024, to households that receive SNAP benefits but do not receive benefits through the Low-Income Energy Assistance Program (LEAP). This fuel assistance program is titled Energy Electronic Benefits Transfer (E-EBT). SNAP households that receive E-EBT will be assigned the highest standard utility allowance in their SNAP budget, which may increase their monthly SNAP allotment. E-EBT provides one-time $21 cash assistance for energy costs
citation_label: OM-FEA-2025-0002 Energy EBT Implementation 1
heading: Page 1

metadata.title: OM-FEA-2025-0002 Energy EBT Implementation

body:
1575 Sherman Street, Denver, CO 80203 P 303.866.5700 cdhs.colorado.gov Jared Polis, Governor | Michelle Barnes, Executive Director to eligible SNAP households. A SNAP household cannot receive E-EBT more than one time in a 12-month period. If a household applies for SNAP, is approved on or after February 8, 2025, and is eligible for E- EBT, they will receive E-EBT at the same time as their first SNAP benefit. For ongoing SNAP cases, eligible households will automatically receive E-EBT in March 2025. CBMS logic will assign the Heating and Cooling Utility Allowance (HCUA) to the household’s SNAP budget beginning in the same month in which the household received E-EBT. The HCUA will remain in place until the household’s next recertification or periodic report. Moving forward, eligibility for E-EBT will be automatically determined at the time of a household’s SNAP application, recertification, or periodic report. If a client has a question about E-EBT, counties can refer clients to visit cdhs.colorado.gov/energy-ebt, email CDHS_energyebt@state.co.us, or call 800.536.5298. Attachments N/A Supersedes N/A Contact For more information about this memo, please contact the E-EBT team at CDHS_energyebt@state.co.us.

Operation memos are active for three years; then they are archived and re-issued if necessary. Access the CDHS Memo Series at cdhs.colorado.gov/our-partners/counties/cdhs-memo-series.

heading: OM-FEA-2025-0003 SNAP Work Requirements Oral Explanation Waiver Extension Approval

metadata.title: OM-FEA-2025-0003 SNAP Work Requirements Oral Explanation Waiver Extension Approval

body:
1575 Sherman Street, Denver, CO 80203 P 303.866.5700 cdhs.colorado.gov Jared Polis, Governor | Michelle Barnes, Executive Director

OPERATION MEMO

Title: SNAP Work Requirements Oral Explanation Waiver Extension Approval Office/Division: Office of Economic Security/Food and Energy Assistance Memo number: OM-FEA-2025-0003 Program area: SNAP Outcome: Notify counties of the extended approval of the waiver that allows for the oral explanation of SNAP work requirements to be waived at recertifications when no interview is required. Pertinent statute/rule: 10 CCR 2506-1 Keywords: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, SNAP, work requirements, federal waiver, Consolidated Work Notice Issue date: March 24, 2025 Effective: January 1, 2025 Expires: December 31, 2025

Intended recipients This communication will be sent to all local office human service directors. Please forward pertinent information to staff members as you deem necessary. Purpose For local office human services department understanding of the approved waiver that allows Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) eligibility workers to waive the oral explanation of work requirements at recertifications that do not require an interview. Action Local offices will adapt their recertification processing standards to remove the expectation that eligibility workers orally explain SNAP work requirements when a recertification interview is not required. SNAP work requirements must still be orally explained to households at initial application and recertification interviews, if the household reports a change that affects the work registration status of a household member, or if a household member asks questions about work requirements. The Consolidated Work Notice will continue to be sent out with all Notices of Action, satisfying the regulatory expectation that a written explanation of work requirements be provided.

citation_label: SNAP Outreach
heading: SNAP Outreach

metadata.title: SNAP Outreach

body: The Colorado SNAP Outreach program, which is administered by the Colorado Department of Human Services, aims to increase access to the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) for eligible individuals and families across Colorado. SNAP Outreach seeks to inform, assist and empower individuals in navigating the SNAP application process through strategic partnerships with local, community-based organizations, targeted outreach efforts, and educational campaigns. SNAP is the most extensive nutritional assistance program in the nation, and CDHS is committed to using innovative and authentic outreach approaches for fighting food insecurity in Colorado.
citation_label: SNAP Outreach 1
heading: SNAP Outreach

metadata.title: SNAP Outreach

body:
The Colorado Department of Human Services partners with community-based organizations to offer SNAP Outreach statewide. Visit their websites to learn more about them.

Read a text description of the SNAP Outreach partner map . Click on the map to view a PDF version.
citation_label: SNAP Outreach 2
heading: SNAP Outreach community partners

metadata.title: SNAP Outreach

body:
Benefits in Action is a one-stop shop for Coloradans seeking support with vital programs like food assistance. The Benefits in Action team provides outreach and education at resource and health fairs, group presentations to potential clients and client-facing staff, and shares program information digitally and in print. Benefits in Action aims to meet Coloradans where they are, keeping in mind their personal challenges and barriers, to help them better understand and utilize SNAP and other programs they need to thrive.

View a map of counties where Benefits in Action operates . (Read a text description of the map.)
citation_label: SNAP Outreach 3
heading: Benefits in Action

metadata.title: SNAP Outreach

body:
Hunger Free Colorado connects people to food resources and federal nutrition programs, such as SNAP, and can refer people to other programs through a statewide multilingual hotline and on-site assistance. Hunger Free Colorado aims to meet existing needs and drives policy, systems and social change to end hunger. Hunger Free Colorado envisions a day when every Coloradan has equitable access to the nutritious food needed to thrive and reach their full potential.

View a map of counties where Hunger Free Colorado operates . (Read a text description of the map.)

body: The SNAP Toolkit is designed to help share information about SNAP benefits with those who are eligible but may not be participating. It also helps let potential partners and the public know how SNAP participation helps the local economy and addresses the national health problem of food insecurity.

citation_label: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26 2
heading: Page 2

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26

body: 2 2. Statement of Need The Colorado Department of Human Services (CDHS) oversees the management and administration of the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP). SNAP is the most extensive nutritional assistance program in the nation, and CDHS is committed to providing the program to those individuals that are eligible and in need of this key strategy for fighting food insecurity in Colorado. CDHS is also working to increase public awareness by reaching out to households that may be eligible for SNAP benefits. The main approach CDHS has is to form partnerships with strategic agencies (SNAP Outreach Partners) that have the experience and training to provide SNAP application assistance. SNAP Outreach partners maintain strong, trusted relationships with a range of Community-Based Organizations (CBOs), referred to as subrecipients in this plan, that are uniquely positioned to reach communities across the state who may otherwise be missed by traditional outreach efforts. Each CBO brings specialized programs and deep community connections that allow for more targeted and effective engagement. These partnerships play a critical role in ensuring that eligible individuals and families, regardless of location, language, or life circumstances, are aware of and able to access SNAP benefits According to Feeding America, in 2023, one in eight Coloradans are struggling with hunger. There are over 744,950 people facing hunger in Colorado, with just under 172,540 of them being children.1 On average, between 2021 and 2023, 11% of Colorado kids lived in households that were food-insecure at some point during the year. From 2021 to 2023, the cost of living increased by 6.77% and between 2023 and 2024 the price of food rose to 2%.2 The share of Colorado households with kids where children were not eating enough because food was unaffordable ranged from 18% in June 2020 to a high of 37% in December 2021-January 2022.3 These numbers underscore the ongoing and urgent need to strengthen food assistance programs like SNAP and expand outreach efforts across the state. Disparities exist for people of color as well as those living in rural and frontier areas. Those who identify as American Indian Alaska Native, Black or African American, Multi racial, or Hispanic, as well as individuals born outside of the U.S., and people living in rural and frontier areas are more likely to experience poverty than the general population of Colorado. Veterans, college students, and older adults are experiencing food and housing insecurity at rising rates. These populations have some unique needs that should be prioritized in the FFY26 plan. To be effective this federal fiscal year, SNAP Outreach efforts will need to use innovative strategies and engage with broad range subrecipients to better serve these communities. There has been an increase in SNAP enrollment from 584,416 in 2024 to 615,319 Coloradans are enrolled in SNAP in 20254 While SNAP participation among low-income individuals increased 1 Feeding America. (2023). Colorado. Retrieved July 22, 2025, from Feedingamerica.org website: https://map.feedingamerica.org/county/2023/overall/colorado 2 Kids Count in Colorado 2025 data book. (2025). In Colorado Kids. Retrieved from https://www.coloradokids.org/wp- content/uploads/2025/05/KIDS-COUNT-2025-ENG-PDF-LINKS-5.28.25.pdf 3 Healthy Food for Denver’s Kids. (2022). Retrieved from https://denvergov.org/files/assets/public/v/1/public-health- and-environment/documents/cbh/healthy-food-for-denvers-kids/2022-reports/hfdk-report_year2-final-508_1.pdf 4 SUPPLEMENTAL NUTRITION ASSISTANCE PROGRAM: NUMBER OF PERSONS PARTICIPATING
citation_label: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26 3
heading: Page 3

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26

body: 3 from 78% in 2022 to 84% in 2023 there is still a gap in eligible individuals and those receiving benefits.5 With the increase in inflation, and the resulting increase in food costs, programs like SNAP are even more important to those in need. CDHS understands that the nutritional and economic benefits of SNAP participation will strengthen the lives of Coloradans. We will continue our mission to provide assistance to those people who need the food support offered as part of SNAP. SNAP Outreach is a collaborative partnership. CDHS will continue to join efforts with counties, community partners, advocates, and other agencies to provide outreach throughout Colorado communities. 3. Outreach Plan Summaries Executive Summary The Colorado Outreach Plan for Federal Fiscal Year (FFY) 2026 has six main objectives that will be met through two partner agencies: Benefits in Action and Hunger Free Colorado. To ensure the most vulnerable populations are targeted and that we are aligning with FNS SNAP Outreach priority areas, outreach will be conducted and prioritized to include hard to reach populations such as Able-Bodied Adults without Dependents (ABAWDS), older adults, students attending institutions of higher education, under-employed households, veterans and communities of color. Both outreach partners are expanding in FFY 26 upon previous years’ successful partnerships with varied CBOs to ensure we accomplish our goal of reaching and providing adapted services to all SNAP eligible populations in Colorado. We expect our work to be community-informed and driven by community events, partnerships with health centers, food banks, churches, higher education institutions, counties, and organizations devoted to serving the community, among others. Additionally, we will ensure the program design, language, and structure authentically meets the needs of the SNAP eligible community, and that we are reaching those who have been marginalized and most impacted by food and nutrition insecurity. CDHS continues to remain committed to our outreach partners and sub-recipients by providing many virtual technical assistance sessions and other in-person engagement opportunities to ensure we can continue to help communities navigate the SNAP program. CDHS is ensuring all partners and sub-recipients are trained in telephonic application assistance and monitor these processes regularly to ensure accuracy in the recordings. We expect to continue offering telephone application assistance and will continue to have opportunities for in-person outreach and application assistance in FFY 26. Last year we made a request in the plan for Outreach Partners and subrecipients to make Change Reports when SNAP enrolled participants make a request. This year, change reports will remain a key component of the plan. We will also set a goal and track the number of change reports submitted for FY26. This effort will help SNAP-enrolled Coloradans maintain their benefits more consistently, while also reducing the volume of calls counties receive for these requests. PEAK Pro provides our partners and subrecipients with a streamlined tool to assist clients in submitting change reports efficiently. CDHS remains committed to expanding on-the-ground application assistance in rural areas and with other hard-to-reach populations. As part of this effort, CDHS is encouraging larger partner agencies to engage and support smaller subrecipients, especially in areas where there has been little to no direct SNAP outreach. This strategy aims to build local capacity, strengthen community trust, (Data as of. (2025). Retrieved from https://fns-prod.azureedge.us/sites/default/files/resource-files/snap-persons-7.pdf 5 SNAP Program Access Index | Food and Nutrition Service. (2023). Retrieved from www.fns.usda.gov website: https://www.fns.usda.gov/snap/qc/pai
citation_label: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26 4
heading: Page 4

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26

body: 4 and ensure that more eligible individuals in hard-to-reach communities can access and maintain SNAP benefits. The five main objectives of the 2026 SNAP Outreach plan are: 1. Provide telephonic and in-person application assistance to submit 26,106 SNAP applications, recertifications, and periodic reports. In addition, assist with the submission of 2,189 change reports. 2. Leverage data and innovative outreach strategies to identify and engage potentially eligible individuals within hard-to-reach populations. 3. Coordinate closely with county SNAP offices and relevant FNS programs to ensure that eligible individuals are enrolled in SNAP or maintain continuous enrollment, while also increasing awareness of other available federal programs. 4. Deliver comprehensive training to community partners in targeted regions on SNAP outreach, basic eligibility criteria, application assistance using PEAK or paper forms, and effective methods for evaluating and reporting program outcomes. 5. SNAP Outreach Partners will continue to partner with at least five sub-recipients across the state in FFY 2026 to expand SNAP outreach, increase program impact, and enhance access to nutrition assistance for eligible populations. 6. SNAP Outreach Partners will work collaboratively with CDHS to explore and develop innovative strategies that promote the long-term sustainability of the SNAP Outreach program, including identifying diversified funding sources and partnership opportunities. Application assistance will continue to consist of providing information on the application process, basic SNAP pre-screening and eligibility criteria, where to apply, and guidance to dispel common misconceptions about the program that may contribute to individuals choosing not to seek assistance. County offices will benefit from the SNAP Outreach Program by receiving more complete applications with supporting documentation and decreased general program questions. Outreach partners will be able to provide clients with basic verification requirements and collect paperwork that may be needed. SNAP Outreach partners will also provide the ability to do Change Reports. This will decrease the county workload in helping clients to change personal information such as, name, phone number, email, etc., when clients need to make a change. Additionally, having outreach partners who are dedicated to assisting those who may have already failed utilizing the online PEAK or another method, increases the likelihood that SNAP participants will remain on SNAP and experience fewer disruptions, thus also benefiting county workloads. To increase SNAP outreach throughout Colorado, both partners will provide outreach training and funding to subrecipients. We have set a state goal for each partner to work with at least five subrecipients, but this goal will be largely exceeded in 2026. It is projected that both partners will work with more than 57 subrecipients. The partners will conduct ongoing technical assistance sessions to empower other partner agencies to convey the advantages of the SNAP program to potentially eligible persons within their own communities. Extending the range of SNAP outreach to other organizations will ensure that SNAP information and application assistance (either in- person or over the phone) is being provided to a broad range of individuals and that we are reaching all SNAP eligible populations. Outreach efforts will be measured utilizing reports from Colorado’s online application system, PEAK. The PEAK reports will consist of the following information: ●Number of new applications submitted. ●Number of change reports when adding a program submitted. ●Number of change reports, all other changes, submitted. ●Number of renewals/recertifications submitted. ●Number of periodic report forms submitted.
citation_label: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26 5
heading: Page 5

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26

body: 5 In addition to the routine reports available through PEAK, outreach partners in partnership with CDHS will continue to utilize the electronic monthly reporting and evaluation system implemented in FFY 24 to make sure key data is being gathered to measure program outcomes. Partners are asked to include information about training, working with subrecipients, outreach attendance at events, distribution of SNAP informational materials, and collect success stories. Here are examples of the information that is collected: ●Number of SNAP in person and telephonic signature applications completed. ●Number of Change Reports and recertification completed. ●Number of periodic report forms submitted. ●Number of SNAP outreach materials distributed. ●Number of outreach presentations, SNAP education and/or training completed. ●Number of events where basic SNAP information was shared with the public. ●Number of subrecipients and the hard-to-reach populations they serve. ●Number of clicks and engagement in social media for outreach efforts. ●Qualitative insights into how SNAP outreach impacts individuals. This information will be used to not only measure the effectiveness of outreach efforts, but also to determine trends in program access and where additional efforts may need to be targeted. To support the achievement of Goals Two and Six of the plan, CDHS will focus this year on developing a data dashboard and an evidence-based portfolio. These tools will enhance program monitoring and provide outreach partners with objective, actionable insight highlighting which strategies are most effective and identifying areas for improvement to better achieve program outcomes. The evidence-based portfolio will consolidate key information about SNAP Outreach efforts in Colorado, as well as best practices and implementation strategies from other states. This resource will also support partners in pursuing additional funding and long-term program sustainability. Finally, the CDHS SNAP Outreach team, along with staff from SNAP Outreach partner organizations, will continue to actively participate in the State Nutrition Action Council (SNAC) and other collaborative initiatives across Colorado. These efforts are aimed at fostering strong, trust-based relationships among Food and Nutrition Service (FNS) programs, promoting coordinated referrals, and ensuring efficient cross-program collaboration while minimizing duplication of efforts. For FFY 26, Colorado will work with two SNAP Outreach providers: Benefits in Action (BIA) and Hunger Free Colorado (HFC). CDHS and outreach partners remain committed to providing SNAP outreach to all SNAP eligible individuals in Colorado. All outreach partners and their subrecipients have trusted ties in the community and capacities to provide in-person application assistance when requested, making our outreach model flexible and responsive to community needs. Benefits in Action The Benefits in Action SNAP Outreach Plan for the period of October 1, 2025, through September 30, 2026, will focus on supporting individuals who face barriers to accessing SNAP benefits. Services will include assistance with submitting initial applications, change reports, recertifications, and periodic reports. Outreach efforts will prioritize individuals who are historically underserved or who encounter obstacles such as limited transportation, lack of information or understanding about program eligibility, or other systemic and logistical challenges. During this period, Benefits in Action anticipates submitting approximately 6,109 SNAP
citation_label: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26 6
heading: Page 6

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26

body: 6 applications. Additionally, affiliated partner organizations (subrecipients) are projected to submit 4,855 SNAP applications over the same timeframe. Key strategies include: ● Provide in-person and telephonic SNAP application assistance. ● Collaborate with county SNAP offices. By working closely, BIA aims to support human service eligibility staff by providing application assistance and reducing churn related to household benefits ending and needing to return to their county office. ● Utilize data and innovative strategies for targeted SNAP outreach, activities, education, and marketing. ● Collaborate with School Districts to reach more SNAP eligible families. ● Conduct targeted outreach activities for hard-to-reach communities and underserved counties throughout the state. These activities include co-location at community organizations serving the targeted communities and hiring bilingual staff who speak the language of the targeted communities. ● Train community partners and subrecipients on effective SNAP outreach techniques. The Senior Manager of Navigation meets with subrecipients monthly to discuss and share ideas and problem solve. ● To expand their reach, BIA will continue partnerships and provide oversight and support to at least ten sub-recipients. ● Create and distribute outreach materials in multiple languages and employ bilingual navigation staff. ● Collaborate with other FNS/Federal programs and maintain their robust in-house referral process. These programs include WIC and several nutrition programs that BIA deploys from their food delivery program. BIA supports Summer EBT, meals for students, and older adult commodities. Benefits in Action (BIA) will use the CDHS-approved monthly outreach reporting form to track key metrics, including the number of eligible individuals reached, events attended to provide SNAP education and information, total outreach contacts made, and SNAP applications submitted. To support accurate and comprehensive data collection, BIA will utilize multiple sources, including PEAK Pro reports, internal paper application tracking, and reports generated from the Benefits in Action database. In addition, BIA receives monthly reports from its subrecipients and will ensure that all required data is consistently collected and submitted in alignment with CDHS reporting guidelines. This multi-source approach will help BIA monitor outreach performance, assess impact, and inform ongoing program improvements. Program Objectives for FY 2026: 1. By September 30, 2026, submit at least 10,964 SNAP applications to Colorado county human services offices. This application goal includes both direct in-person and telephonic application assistance completed by BIA and sub-recipient agencies. 2. By September 30, 2026, BIA and their subs will submit 541 change reports. 3. By September 30, 2026, provide SNAP information to at least 18,000 SNAP recipients or potential recipients. 4. By September 30, 2026, attend 510 SNAP information and outreach events. 5. Commit to provide oversight and monitoring for at least ten subrecipients including ECHO, The Fax, A Little Help, C3, CAFI, Peak Pediatrics, The Village Initiative, Lakewood Police Department Navigators, Care & Share, Westside Cares, C4, Kavod Senior Care, and Neighbor Works. BIA will continue to work with other agencies interested in conducting SNAP Outreach on behalf of Benefits in Action. 6. Submit a final report by December 1, 2026.
citation_label: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26 7
heading: Page 7

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26

body: 7 Benefits in Action (BIA) has developed a comprehensive outreach plan designed to increase both awareness of and access to SNAP benefits among eligible Coloradans. By leveraging their strong network of community partnerships and with continued support from CDHS, BIA is well- positioned to deliver targeted, effective outreach across the state. Their approach includes on- the-ground assistance, collaboration with subrecipients, and data-driven strategies to reach underserved and hard-to-reach populations. BIA is confident that these coordinated efforts will ensure reliable SNAP outreach coverage throughout Federal Fiscal Year 2026. Hunger Free Colorado Hunger Free Colorado is committed to connecting eligible Coloradans to SNAP via their SNAP Outreach work. This comes in the form of partnerships, collaborations, and commitments. Hunger Free Colorado will accomplish this work via the two programs funded through SNAP Outreach: SNAP PEAS and SNAP Outreach: ▪ SNAP PEAS (Partners Engaged in Application Assistance) – SNAP enrollment training and on-going support for organizations across the state, providing funding for them as sub-recipients, hosting Peak Pro licenses, facilitating telephonic signatures, overseeing compliance, and providing ongoing comprehensive training via the Online Learning Platform. ▪ SNAP Outreach – Hybrid approach of hotline and in person mobile outreach. ▪ Hotline & Referrals – Statewide bilingual food resource hotline taking cold calls and conducting active outreach and follow-ups based on referrals from community partners, such as hospitals, clinics, and physicians. Utilizing PEAKPro, HFC assists in SNAP application services. ▪ Mobile Outreach – On-site SNAP application assistance via PEAKPro at libraries, drop- in centers, county offices, clinics and other sites frequented by low-income communities. These strategies allow HFC to provide support in innovative ways to targeted regions. The HFC Hotline is statewide and allows staff to connect with those who are unable to travel to a physical location for reasons outside of their control (such as lack of transportation or mobility challenges). Many Coloradans experience the divide in digital access, and some lack fluency in technology. As a phone-based resource, HFC is also able to assist everyone, despite their situation. All their hybrid Hotline and Mobile Outreach navigators and specialists are fluent in Spanish. For individuals who are not English or Spanish speaking, HFC staff uses TransPerfect for interpretation. When the team members are providing mobile outreach, they are connecting with people who are already arriving at various services and locations, such as hospitals and libraries, which creates a broader reach for connection. Data HFC collects a wide variety of data which, when analyzed, helps them determine how to better engage SNAP eligible individuals, especially in hard-to-reach populations. The SNAP Outreach team records all hotline/mobile outreach and partner events into Salesforce and will continue to do so at a 95%-100% accuracy rate as evidenced by case and record review. Additionally, HFC will continue to use Power BI to assist in visualizing trends and identifying gaps in outreach strategy. HFC’s Client Services Data Workgroup (CSDW) is a formalized working space where various departments come together to influence the direction of HFC’s outreach strategies. This workgroup will reconvene, after a short summer break, in October of 2025. The goals of this workgroup are to improve data analysis and collection, understand trends in SNAP Outreach and hard-to reach- populations to increase outreach efforts and success. Further, this group will continue to support and fine tune client surveys and client stories collection. Currently, HFC conducts short post- interaction surveys to gauge satisfaction, capture barriers, and collect success stories for program
citation_label: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26 8
heading: Page 8

metadata.title: Colorado SNAP Annual Plan FY26

body: 8 improvement and accuracy. Additionally, HFC has deployed targeted follow-up surveys for adults age 60+ and households that are prenatal up to having children of 3 years of age. These surveys focus on tech, mobility, and health-specific obstacles. Analysis of these surveys is leading to the goal of focusing closer on these obstacles for all SNAP eligible Coloradans. County Coordination HFC navigators and specialists will accurately gather all important case details, including the case number, county of residence and a clear description of the client issue before determining if an escalation is warranted. Should an escalation be warranted, HFC will escalate the issue to the assigned county using Salesforce and PEAKPro data. All escalated cases will be monitored closely with follow-up within 7 business days. All escalated client cases will remain open in Salesforce until a response is received from the county or until a verifiable update is reflected in PEAKPro. Other FNS Referrals HFC will ensure clients have access to information on all available support services provided by FNS. Navigators and specialists will assess the individual's needs during interactions and provide information or make adequate referrals to relevant federal programs. Referrals will be completed in a timely manner, with follow-up as needed by the navigator to confirm connection to services. Any shared information or referral made will be clearly documented in the client’s case in Salesforce. Comprehensive Training HFC will deliver comprehensive training to over 200 navigators at 46 statewide community partners in Colorado on SNAP Outreach, eligibility criteria, application assistance, and PEAK Pro through an online learning platform, as well as effective methods for evaluating and reporting program outcomes, from October 2025 through September 2026. Within the learning platform, there are quizzes and knowledge checks that simultaneously provide HFC with information on the progress their subrecipients are making and gaps in their learning. Their SNAP PEAS navigators include individuals from various linguistic, learning, and cultural backgrounds, and they created the platform with this in mind. The learning modules offer various ways to absorb learning content, including videos, audio, visuals, and written text. Additionally, the entire platform is available in Spanish to make learning more accessible for the numerous navigators whose preferred language is Spanish. Their learning modules are designed to cater to most learning styles and are presented in bite-sized pieces, allowing individuals to complete the training at their own pace. Most participants complete the training within a week. SNAP PEAS In FFY 2026, HFC will collaborate with 47 subrecipients (SNAP PEAS) across Colorado to expand SNAP outreach, increase program impact, and improve access to nutrition assistance for eligible populations. Each partnership is established through an individual contract between HFC and the subrecipient, with funding provided by HFC. Grants are awarded based on each subrecipient’s capacity, the populations and communities they serve, and their geographic location. These grants are tied to annual application goals, which are determined by the amount of funding received. The contracts outline all key details, including the budget, allocated funds, application targets, and compliance requirements. The contract also provides access to PEAK Pro Licenses, which is necessary for assisting clients with SNAP applications. CDHS Collaboration

body: 9 Alongside CDHS, HFC will explore and develop innovative strategies that promote long-term sustainability of the SNAP Outreach program. HFC will identify funding opportunities that support these efforts and present them to CDHS for approval for collaboration. Program Objectives for FY 2026: 1. Hunger Free Colorado will provide telephonic and in-person application assistance to submit 15,142 SNAP applications, recertifications, and periodic reports. In addition, HFC will assist with the submission of 1,648 change reports. 2. By leveraging data collected via Salesforce entries, Client Surveys, and staff feedback, alongside innovative outreach strategies, HFC will identify and engage eligible individuals, specifically in hard-to-reach populations such as rural areas. 3. HFC will coordinate closely with county SNAP offices and relevant FNS programs to ensure that eligible individuals are enrolled in SNAP and maintain continuous enrollment, while also increasing awareness of other available federal programs. 4. HFC will deliver comprehensive training to community partners in targeted regions on SNAP outreach, basic eligibility criteria, application assistance using PEAK or other forms, and effective methods for evaluating and reporting program outcomes. 5. HFC will partner with 47 subrecipients (SNAP PEAS) across the state of Colorado in FFY 2026 to expand SNAP Outreach, increase program impact, and enhance access to nutrition assistance for eligible populations. 6. HFC will work collaboratively with CDHS to explore and develop innovative strategies that promote the long-term sustainability of the SNAP Outreach program, including identifying diverse funding sources and partnership opportunities.
